topic: Why Cast Iron Pans Get Better With Age

packaging.title: Why Cast Iron Gets Slicker the More You Use It
altTitles: The Real Reason Old Cast Iron Beats New
How Cast Iron Becomes Nonstick On Its Own
packaging.description: A food science look at why a well-used cast iron skillet turns glassy and nonstick over time, the polymerized oil layer that builds up, and why brand-new pans never cook as well as your grandmother's.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A glossy black cast iron skillet at an angle with a fried egg sliding cleanly off its mirror-slick surface

packaging.hookPromise: Your grandmother's beat-up skillet cooks better than a $200 new one, and the reason is a layer of cooked oil that's literally turned into plastic on the metal.
openLoops: Why does a brand-new cast iron pan stick when an old one doesn't?
What is actually happening to the oil every time you heat the pan?
Why does washing it with soap NOT ruin it, despite what everyone says?
packaging.payoff: Repeated heating polymerizes the oil into a hard, smooth plastic-like coating bonded to the iron — the same chemistry as drying paint and varnish — so the 'seasoning' is a real engineered surface that genuinely improves with every cook.
packaging.whyItWorks: Nearly everyone owns or has heard about cast iron and wondered why old ones are prized, and 'cooked oil becomes plastic' is a counterintuitive payoff worth repeating.
